hero.subtitle: We use Augmented Reality with WebXR and ARCore to visualize archaeological findings in their original context. Open source technology, made by students.

problem.label: The problem
problem.title: We only see stones
problem.desc_1: When we visit an archaeological site, a museum or an interpretation center, we often only see stones and ruins. Everything must remain unaltered for conservation. But with Augmented Reality we can overlay 3D reconstructions, information panels and animations over real space, enriching the experience in an educational way without touching or altering anything.
problem.desc_2: Our open AR experiences platform allows any museum or site to upload and share educational AR content: 3D reconstructions, information panels and animations overlaid on real space.
problem.ar_realtime_desc: Point your Android and the findings appear overlaid on the real site
problem.artifact_analysis_desc: 3D reconstruction of fragmented pieces with origin and dating data
problem.scan_desc: We capture the space with Scaniverse creating a millimetric 3D mesh
problem.visualize_desc: WebXR anchors objects to the real world without apps, just Chrome and ARCore
problem.share_desc: All code is open source so any team can use and improve it

showcase.label: Our vision
showcase.title: The archaeology of the future
showcase.desc: This is how Augmented Reality can transform the way we explore, document and share archaeological heritage.
showcase.item1_desc: Visualize what the original buildings looked like overlaid on the current ruins.
showcase.item2_desc: Imagine what daily life was like in the Roman baths with life-size holographic figures.
showcase.item3_desc: Access data, topographic maps and records of each piece directly at the site.

timeline.label: The process
timeline.title: We didn't get here in a straight line
timeline.desc: We tried 5 technologies before finding the solution. This is what we learned.
timeline.step1_desc: Objects stuck to screen, no real anchoring
timeline.step2_desc: Only works with native apps, not web
timeline.step5_desc: Native tracking with ARCore on Chrome, no server

architecture.label: Open Source
architecture.title: Open code for you to improve
architecture.desc: All our architecture is documented and available. We don't want this project to die here. We want other teams to replicate it, improve it and take it further.

replicate.label: Replicate the experiment
replicate.title: Do it yourself too
replicate.step1: Scan the space with Scaniverse → export as .glb
replicate.step2: Download our code → upload your GLB file
replicate.step3: Open the URL in Chrome → tap the floor → AR objects appear anchored to the space
replicate.tech_button: 🔬 See the technology behind the project

team.label: Who we are
team.title: The team behind the project
team.desc: We are students from Maker Lab / Espacio Vivo (Ferrol, Galicia). We participate in the FIRST LEGO League 2026. If you want to replicate the project or have questions, write to us.

footer.project_info: FLL 2026 Project — Maker Lab · Espacio Vivo · Ferrol, Galicia

community.title: Sites with AR
community.desc: Explore archaeological sites that use our Augmented Reality technology. Know one? Register it and share AR content with the community!

community.cta_title: Know a site or interpretation center?
community.cta_desc: Register it on the platform and share AR content with the community. Anyone can add a new site with photos, videos and 3D models.
community.no_ar_content: No AR content for this site yet.

tech.hero_title: The technology behind our AR project
tech.hero_desc: Complete guide to understand how the Augmented Reality we built for archaeology works. With real code examples.
tech.stack_title: How does the final solution work?
tech.stack_desc: After testing 5 different technologies, we arrived at an elegant architecture that uses the Android device's hardware directly, without external servers.
tech.flow1: The user opens the website in Chrome — no installation needed
tech.flow2: The browser accesses the device's native tracking: camera + gyroscope + sensors
tech.flow4: 3D mesh of real space generated with Scaniverse
tech.flow5: The user taps the floor → the object appears in exact position and doesn't move
tech.path_title: Our path: from failure to success
tech.path_desc: We didn't get here in a straight line. Each tool we tried taught us something. This is the real timeline.
tech.phase1_body: We started with this commercial WebAR platform. The problem: objects were "stuck to the screen" like a sticker on the camera. They didn't know where the phone was in real space.
tech.phase1_lesson: Lesson: We needed a technology that understood the phone's position in space.
tech.phase2_body: Professional visual positioning platform. Only works with Unity (native app). For us, it was essential that it worked without apps.
tech.phase3_title: Phase 3 — Own server with synthetic descriptors
tech.phase3_body: We generated virtual images from the LiDAR point cloud to create ORB descriptors. 15,000 descriptors generated, 0 matches with real photos.
tech.phase4_title: Phase 4 — Own server with real photos
tech.phase4_body: 10 real photos → 112,000 ORB descriptors. The phone sent frames via WebSocket, the server did matching with solvePnPRansac.
tech.phase4_issue2: False positives at 100% confidence with incorrect poses
tech.phase4_issue3: OpenCV→Three.js coordinate conversion was a nightmare
tech.phase4_issue4: Objects appeared for a second and disappeared
tech.phase5_body: The key insight: the phone already knows where it is. It has camera, gyroscope, accelerometer and depth sensors. We used Chrome's WebXR API. No server, no tunnel, no coordinate conversion.
tech.webxr_desc: WebXR is the browser API that enables creating Augmented Reality experiences directly in the browser. No apps, no installations.
tech.webxr_hw_desc: Talks directly to sensors: camera, gyroscope, accelerometer. Accesses the device's AR hardware.
tech.webxr_hit_desc: Enables raycast against the real world to detect surfaces and get their exact coordinates.
tech.webxr_pose_desc: Each frame, WebXR provides the device's exact position and orientation in 3D space.
tech.webxr_why_title: Why do we use Chrome on Android?
tech.webxr_why_body: Chrome uses Google's ARCore underneath. ARCore combines camera, gyroscope and depth sensors to create a real-time 3D map of the environment. Works on most modern Android devices.
tech.webxr_compat: Works on Chrome (Android) and Safari (iOS). Firefox doesn't support WebXR AR yet.
tech.three_desc: Three.js is the JavaScript library that renders 3D objects overlaid on reality.
tech.three_scene: The 'world' where all 3D objects live. Like a film studio set.
tech.three_camera: The 'eye' that sees the scene. In AR, synced with the device's real camera.
tech.three_renderer: Converts 3D scene to pixels. Made transparent so the AR camera shows behind.
tech.three_mesh: A 3D object = Geometry (shape) + Material (appearance). E.g.: sphere + gold = pottery.
tech.three_glb: Standard format for web 3D models. Scaniverse exports in GLB.
tech.three_math_desc: All 3D position/rotation is math. WebXR gives us these matrices directly.
tech.lidar_desc: Depth sensors measure distances to understand space geometry. Scaniverse uses them to create 3D models.
tech.lidar_how_desc: Thousands of measurements per second. Calculating exact distances to each point in the environment.
tech.lidar_scaniverse_desc: App that uses the device's sensors to create photorealistic 3D scans.
tech.lidar_glb_desc: Scaniverse exports as .glb — standard format for web 3D models. Three.js loads it directly.
tech.lidar_insight_body: Before, we tried calculating position by comparing photos with the 3D map on a Python server. The solution: use the GLB only as a visual reference. Tracking is done by WebXR with ARCore in real time.
tech.lidar_step1_desc: Move slowly. LiDAR captures the geometry.
tech.lidar_step3_desc: Export as .glb → upload to web server.
tech.lidar_step4_desc: Three.js loads the GLB as a semi-transparent mesh.
tech.opencv_desc: In phases 3 and 4, we tried to build our own visual positioning system. We failed, but learned a lot.
tech.opencv_orb_body: ORB detects 'interesting' points in an image (corners, edges) and creates a unique mathematical vector describing them.
tech.opencv_fail2_desc: solvePnP returned 100% confidence with incorrect poses.
tech.opencv_fail3_desc: OpenCV (Y↓) vs Three.js (Y↑). Objects mirrored or underground.
tech.opencv_solution_desc: Let the iPhone do tracking via hardware. WebXR + ARKit + LiDAR.
tech.g_webxr: Browser API for AR/VR. Accesses device tracking without apps.
tech.g_hittest: Detect where an imaginary ray hits the real world.
tech.g_threejs: JavaScript library for 3D graphics in the browser using WebGL.
tech.g_lidar: Sensor that measures distances with light pulses. Creates 3D point clouds.
tech.g_scaniverse: Free app that uses iPhone's LiDAR to create 3D models.
tech.g_pointcloud: Collection of millions of 3D points representing a space's surface.
tech.g_mesh: Surface of triangles wrapping the point cloud.
tech.g_arkit: Apple's AR framework. WebXR uses it internally in Safari.
tech.g_opencv: Computer vision library. Detects objects, faces, patterns in images.
tech.g_orb: Mathematical vector describing an 'interesting' point in an image.
tech.g_matching: Comparing descriptors from two images to find matches.
tech.g_solvepnp: Algorithm that calculates where the camera was using 3D reference points.
tech.g_ransac: Statistical technique to find the best solution ignoring outliers.
tech.g_websocket: Persistent connection between browser and server.
tech.g_quaternion: 4-component number representing 3D rotations without gimbal lock.
tech.g_pose: Combination of position (x,y,z) + orientation of an object in space.
tech.g_reticle: AR aiming point that follows the floor surface.
tech.g_frame: One image frame. In AR, the loop generates 60 frames per second.
tech.res_webxr: Official WebXR samples collection from Google.
tech.res_threejs: The best Three.js course on the internet, by Bruno Simon.
tech.res_opencv: Official documentation with computer vision examples.
